text: The appendix is visibly protruding into the hernia sac, appearing displaced from its usual anatomical position.
The surgeon demonstrates the adhesions of the appendix, which are visibly sticky and adhered to the iliac vessels.
The surgeon works laterally, using sweeping motions to clear and connect tissue planes, aiming to establish a continuous surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, focusing on the posterior rectus sheath, which is visible along the length of the incision, while avoiding the frozen suprapubic space typically seen post-prostatectomy.
The surgeon attempts to come down directly on top of the visible sac, using sweeping motions to move surrounding tissues laterally.
The surgeon carefully isolates anatomical landmarks, working slowly to identify and delineate structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls the bladder back towards themselves, using the trocar to separate it from the pubic bone.
The surgeon approaches the iliopubic tract, visible in the surgical field, as part of the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon observes the appendix, which remains adhered to surrounding structures, indicating a need for further dissection to free it.
The surgeon carefully sweeps tissue downwards, staying close to the sac to maintain anatomical precision.
The surgeon works directly on the sac, manipulating the tissue to maintain proximity to the anatomical structure.
The surgeon opens the sac, revealing the appendix that is visually stuck within the sac.
The surgeon is positioned close to the appendix, visible from underneath the surgical field, while maintaining proximity to the sac.
The surgeon gently pulls up and twists the tissue to maneuver the appendix, attempting to make it fall away from the surrounding structures.
The appendix comes into view as the surgeon manipulates the surrounding tissue to improve visibility.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures down off the visible sac, manipulating the tissue to separate the anatomical elements.
The surgeon pulls up the appendix, making it visible as it is elevated from its surrounding structures.
The surgeon examines the appendix, which appears fused to the adjacent hernia sac, attempting to understand the cause of this adhesion.
The surgeon decides to remove the appendix, which is visible being manipulated and separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers laterally to avoid disrupting the blood supply to the appendix, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully pulls and manipulates the appendix to establish the location of its blood supply, observing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon visually inspects the spermatic cord, noting its intact condition amidst the surgical field.
The surgeon has isolated the spermatic cord and is proceeding to work medially along its path.
The surgeon performs gentle sweeps with the cautery device, applying a few pulses to the tissue, which visibly separates and falls away from the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls on the tissue, revealing the areolar tissue which becomes visible as it separates from surrounding structures.
The surgeon pushes the tissue flap downward, revealing underlying areolar tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates that the blood supply to the area is preserved, with visible vessels intact and undisturbed.
The surgeon sutures the mesh in place using a standard technique, securing it to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completes the final stitch, securing the mesh in place within the surgical field.
The surgeon runs the suture medially, preparing to address the appendix and the lower flap.
The surgeon splits the flap down the middle of the mesh, extending the incision towards the epigastric vessels.
The surgeon anchors the opening superiorly, allowing the tissue to hold itself up visibly.
The surgeon focuses on the appendix while cinching up surrounding tissues, ensuring proper exposure and closure.
The surgeon observes thickened flaps and hernia sacs, noting the robust blood supply visible within some of these hernia sacs.
The surgeon applies bipolar cautery to a vessel within the thickened flap and hernia sac, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon skives the flap and peritoneal closure while attempting to identify the endpoint of the mesoappendix.
The surgeon skives the tissue flap, attempting to cinch it up, with visible manipulation of the flap in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a large hole in the tissue, which is sufficient for an interparietal hernia, and continues skiving the surrounding tissue.
